mnemonics.title: P-A-S-S Method for Object Passing
mnemonics.content:
P - Pass by value when copying is needed
A - Avoid returning references to locals
S - Slice prevention: use pointers/references for polymorphism
S - Safety: use const reference for read-only access

mnemonics.title: CRAP - Copy/Reference/Avoid/Ptr
mnemonics.content:
C - Copy (use pass-by-value) for small objects or when modification is needed locally
R - Reference (use const&) for large read-only objects
A - Avoid returning refs to local variables
P - Pointer use when NULL check needed or C-interfacing

acronyms.usage: Compiler optimization that avoids copy when returning local objects by value, making return-by-value efficient
Similar to RVO but for named local variables; compiler constructs the object directly in the caller's memory
Pattern where resources are acquired in constructors and released in destructors; crucial when passing objects with dynamic memory
Special constructor that creates a new object as a copy of an existing object; invoked during pass-by-value

visualTips.visualization: Draw a box representing main() with an object inside. Draw an arrow to function() showing either: (1) 'copy' label = pass by value (dashed line, shows copy being made), (2) 'address' label = pass by reference (solid arrow pointing to same object), (3) 'pointer' label = pass by pointer (arrow from pointer box to object)
Draw stack frames as boxes. Show original object in caller's stack. Draw arrow to new copy in called function's stack. Label copy constructor as the bridge between them.
Draw triangle: Top = const Type* (pointer to const) - most flexible, accepts const and non-const. Middle = Type* (regular pointer). Bottom = Type* const (const pointer) - least flexible.

analogies.analogy: Like photocopying a document - you give someone a copy to work with, the original stays safe with you.
Like giving someone your house key - they can access your house directly, and changes they make affect the real house.
Like giving someone a read-only key - they can enter and look around but cannot change anything inside.
Like photographing only the front of a 3D sculpture - you lose all the depth and detail from the sides.
Like having two遥控s pointing to the same TV - pressing one affects the same TV, and you might accidentally turn off someone else's control.
Like keeping a phone number of someone who moved away - when you call, you get a stranger or no answer.
